Maria S. Knirk, JD, MBA — Director, Office of Enforcement, Office of Compliance and Enforcement, Human Foods Program.
www.unitechusk.com.
August 20, 2025 through September 24, 2025.
January 12, 2026 — the response only requested an additional four weeks to respond, and FDA states no further correspondence was received from the firm.
15 working days of receipt of the letter.
Inspection start (August 20, 2025) → Inspection end / FDA 483 issued (September 24, 2025) → Firm's extension request (January 12, 2026) → Warning-letter date (March 20, 2026).
Cardiag; Diabetech; Cleanse-CAMP; MACA; Kidney Cleanse; Pure Pysyllium Husk.
Cardiag, Diabetech, and MACA.
Cardiag, Cleanse-camp, and MACA 1000 mg.
21 CFR 111.70(e); 21 CFR 111.205(a); 21 CFR 111.255(a); and 21 CFR Part 111, Subpart N.
United States Food and Drug Administration, Human Foods Program, Office of Enforcement, 5001 Campus Drive, College Park, Maryland 20740-3835.

Cardiag and MACA. (Diabetech is cited as unapproved + misbranded but not as adulterated dietary supplement; Cleanse-CAMP / Cleanse-camp is cited as unapproved + adulterated dietary supplement but not as misbranded.)

Because they are intended for treatment or prevention of one or more diseases that are not amenable to self-diagnosis, treatment, or prevention without the supervision of a licensed practitioner. It is therefore impossible to write adequate directions for a layperson to use them safely for their intended purposes.

MACA: 'MACA may helps [sic] to reduce erectile dysfunction.' or 'MACA may reduce blood pressure'. Diabetech: 'Helping in daily regulation of blood glucose levels' (or 'Diabetech powder for Diabetics', or 'supports blood sugar control / reduces inflammation / helps enhance insulin sensitivity').

Cleanse-CAMP — 'Relieves Constipation' and '[H]ealthy alternative to over-the-counter laxatives…'. Kidney Cleanse — 'This supplement helps…reduce inflammation'. Pure Psyllium Husk — 'Support to lower LDL Cholesterol'.
No. The firm's January 12, 2026 reply only requested an additional four weeks to respond, and FDA states no further correspondence had been received from the firm.
No. The CGMP / adulterated-dietary-supplement section names only Cardiag, Cleanse-camp, and MACA 1000 mg as examples; Kidney Cleanse is named only as an unapproved new drug.
No. Only Cardiag, Diabetech, and MACA are named in the misbranded-drugs section.
No, not as reported in the letter. The letter says only that the firm requested a four-week extension on January 12, 2026 and that FDA had received no further correspondence.

No. Nupack Inc. is the firm name and the legal recipient; www.unitechusk.com is the website where Nupack takes orders for these products. They refer to the same operation but are distinct identifiers in the letter.
